results.title: ✨ Google AI Overview
results.snippet:
AI Overview
Rust provides several concurrency models designed around the core principle of "Fearless Concurrency," which uses its ownership and type system to prevent data races at compile time. 
1. Multi-Threaded Concurrency (OS Threads) 
Rust supports native operating system threads, often called 1:1 threading. 
Shared State: Managed safely using Mutex (Mutual Exclusion) or RwLock. Rust ensures you cannot access data inside a Mutex without first locking it, and the compiler prevents you from forgetting to unlock it via the MutexGuard scope.
Message Passing: Following the philosophy "Do not communicate by sharing memory; instead, share memory by communicating," Rust uses Channels (specifically mpsc: multi-producer, single-consumer) to send data between threads safely. 
2. Asynchronous Programming (Async/Await) 
The Async/Await model is used for handling a large number of concurrent tasks (like network I/O) on a small number of OS threads. 
Futures: Rust uses Futures, which are objects representing a computation that will finish later.
Cooperative Multitasking: Unlike threads which are preempted by the OS, async tasks must "yield" control back to an Executor (like Tokio) to allow other tasks to run.
Zero-Cost: Asynchronous functions are transformed into state machines at compile time, meaning they carry no runtime overhead like a garbage collector. 
3. Data Parallelism
For CPU-bound tasks, Rust leverages libraries like Rayon to implement data parallelism. 
This model splits a large data set (like an array) into smaller chunks and processes them in parallel across all available CPU cores.
It effectively turns sequential iterators into parallel ones with minimal code changes. 
4. Actor Model (via Crates)
While not built into the standard library, the Actor Model is a popular pattern in the Rust ecosystem. 
Actors: Computation is divided into independent "actors" that communicate solely through message passing.
Safety: Since actors do not share state, this model avoids many common synchronization bugs. Common libraries for this include Actix. 
Summary Table: Choosing a Model
\n| Model | Best For | Typical Tool |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| Threads | CPU-heavy tasks with few connections | std::thread, Arc, Mutex |\n| Async | I/O-heavy tasks (web servers, proxies) | async/await, Tokio |\n| Data Parallelism | Massive computations on data sets | Rayon |\n| Actor Model | Distributed systems, complex state | Actix |\n\n
Concurrency Models in Rust. Process & Thread & Async
